script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed one point nine nine percent down on the session.

Sandisk collapsed twelve point six three percent in Tuesday's session, closing at one thousand six hundred seventy-three dollars and ninety-seven cents. That is a drop of two hundred forty-one dollars and ninety-five cents in a single day.

Semiconductors were broadly under pressure Tuesday as profit-taking rotated capital out of chip names and into oversold enterprise software. No major macro prints were on the calendar, but the Nasdaq fell meaningfully on the session, reflecting broad risk-off sentiment concentrated in the technology sector.

The dominant story of Tuesday's session was the semiconductor selloff, and Intel led the primary holdings lower, dropping six point one two percent. But the gravitational center of the move was actually broader — a coordinated retreat across memory, GPU, and chip equipment names. Micron fell four point three two percent, Nvidia dropped three point five two percent, and Advanced Micro Devices shed four point two one percent. The catalyst, according to reporting from Yahoo Finance, was profit-taking rotating into oversold enterprise software names like Elastic and SentinelOne. Separately, ASML — the Dutch chip equipment giant — reports quarterly earnings Wednesday, and traders appeared to de-risk ahead of that print, given the company faces both U.S. export control legislation targeting its China business and questions about capacity outlook. China accounts for an estimated twenty percent of ASML's sales this year.

Nvidia closed at two hundred three dollars and fifty-three cents, down three point five two percent on Tuesday. The stock remains the portfolio's largest holding at seven point six percent, so every move here carries outsized weight. News flow was thin on Nvidia directly, but the ASML earnings due Wednesday are a direct read-through for the entire chip supply chain and should be watched closely by holders here.

Turning to Apple, which closed at three hundred seventeen dollars and thirty-one cents, up zero point six three percent — one of the session's cleaner bright spots. Apple was the only major U.S. tech name to finish in the green alongside Microsoft. In China, IDC data released Tuesday showed Apple posted twenty-four point four percent shipment growth in the second quarter, even as the broader market fell four point three percent for a fifth straight quarterly decline. Separately, Apple sued OpenAI last week over alleged trade secret disclosures involving Apple employees. That legal overhang is worth monitoring.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-two dollars and fifty-one cents, down one point three one percent. The company announced a collaboration with INVIDI Technologies to integrate Google Ad Manager for addressable advertising on unconnected set-top boxes globally — a programmatic expansion targeting households previously unreachable digitally.

Micron closed at nine hundred thirty-seven dollars, down four point three two percent on the session. The move tracked the broader chip selloff, with Yahoo Finance reporting that rotation out of chip stocks drove the afternoon decline. Sandisk's collapse of twelve point six three percent added downward pressure across the memory complex. ASML's Wednesday earnings are the next key catalyst for Micron holders.

Over at Microsoft, the stock closed at three hundred ninety dollars and ninety-nine cents, up one point five three percent — the strongest performer among primary holdings Tuesday. Analyst commentary out Tuesday suggested Microsoft could move sharply higher following its earnings release scheduled for July twenty-ninth, citing AI infrastructure momentum.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-seven dollars and thirty-one cents, up zero point eight zero percent, holding steady as consumer discretionary names generally outperformed the chip complex on the session.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred thirty-four dollars and thirty-nine cents, down four point two one percent. The decline mirrors Nvidia's and is consistent with the broader profit-taking narrative across GPU-exposed names. The next earnings catalyst for Advanced Micro Devices will be the key reset point for that position.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and seventy-six cents, down three point one nine percent Tuesday. No company-specific news drove the move; the decline appears tied to broader risk-off sentiment rather than any fundamental development.

Intel closed at one hundred three dollars and twelve cents, down six point one two percent — the worst performer among primary holdings on Tuesday's session by percentage. Intel's decline outpaced every other primary name and warrants attention heading into the next several sessions. ASML's Wednesday report may offer indirect read-through on the equipment and fab capacity dynamics affecting Intel's roadmap.

Three items to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, ASML quarterly earnings are due Wednesday morning — a direct signal for the entire semiconductor supply chain including Nvidia, Micron, and Advanced Micro Devices. Second, Microsoft earnings are scheduled for July twenty-ninth, with analyst commentary Tuesday flagging meaningful upside potential from AI infrastructure spend. Third, China smartphone market data from IDC will continue to filter through — Apple's twenty-four point four percent shipment growth in Q two is a data point that could attract further analyst revisions.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. ASML's earnings report Wednesday morning is the single most important data point for this portfolio right now — watch that print closely.
